response: The rain slanted cold over the chipped bus-stop bench, where Lila clutched a crumpled library book stamped due three years prior. A man with a scar snaking from his left eyebrow to his jaw sat beside her, holding a tattered copy of the same out-of-print poetry collection. Lila’s thumb brushed the faded coffee stain on her book’s spine—one she’d made at a college open mic, where she’d lent her copy to a boy who’d fled mid-poem after a family emergency. He tapped his book’s matching coffee stain, his voice rough like unfiltered rain: “I’ve carried this to 17 bus stops in three states, hoping to return it.” When the bus rumbled into view, Lila didn’t reach for her fare—instead, she flipped to the dog-eared page where she’d scrawled her phone number all those years ago. The rain softened to mist as he read the number, his scar crinkling into a smile that felt like the home neither had realized they’d been searching for.
